1.text: Fifty-five years had passed since Lehi left Jerusalem. Nephi gave me, Jacob, a commandment about these small plates on which these things are written.
He commanded me, Jacob, to write on these plates a few things I considered most important. He told me to only briefly mention the history of his people, called the people of Nephi.
He said that the detailed history of his people should be written on his other plates, and that I should preserve these plates and hand them down to my descendants from generation to generation.

If there was any sacred preaching, important revelation, or significant prophecy, I should record the main points on these plates and include as much as possible, for Christ's sake and for the sake of our people.
Because of our faith and deep concern, it had truly been revealed to us what things would happen to our people.
We also had many revelations and the spirit of much prophecy, so we knew about Christ and his coming kingdom.

Therefore, we worked hard among our people to persuade them to come to Christ and partake of God's goodness, so they could enter into his rest. We feared that God would swear in his anger that they could not enter in, as the Israelites provoked him during their time in the wilderness.

We wished to God that we could persuade all people not to rebel against God and provoke him to anger, but that all people would believe in Christ and consider his death and bear his cross and the shame of the world. Therefore I, Jacob, took it upon myself to fulfill my brother Nephi's commandment.
Nephi had begun to grow old and knew he would soon die. So he anointed a man to be king and ruler over his people, according to the custom of kings.
The people loved Nephi deeply. He had been a great protector for them, wielding the sword of Laban in their defense and working all his life for their well-being.

The people wanted to remember his name. So whoever ruled after him was called by the people Second Nephi, Third Nephi, and so on, according to the reigns of the kings. The people called them this regardless of their actual names.
Those who were not Lamanites were called Nephites. However, the people were actually called Nephites, Jacobites, Josephites, Zoramites, Lamanites, Lemuelites, and Ishmaelites.

But I, Jacob, will not use all these separate names. I will call Lamanites anyone who seeks to destroy the people of Nephi, and Nephites anyone who is friendly to Nephi, according to the reigns of the kings.
The people of Nephi, under the reign of the second king, began to grow hard in their hearts and engage in wicked practices — like David and Solomon of old, desiring many wives and concubines.
They also began searching for gold and silver, and they began to be lifted up somewhat in pride.
Therefore I, Jacob, spoke these words to them as I taught them in the temple, having first received my assignment from the Lord.
I, Jacob, and my brother Joseph had been consecrated as priests and teachers of this people by Nephi's own hand.

We took our calling from the Lord seriously, accepting the responsibility that if we did not teach the people God's word with all diligence, their sins would be on our heads. Otherwise, their blood would be on our garments, and we would not be found spotless at the last day.

2.text: These are the words that Jacob, Nephi's brother, spoke to the people of Nephi after the death of Nephi:

Now, my beloved people, I, Jacob, feel the responsibility I have before God to honor my calling with seriousness, and to free my garments of your sins. I come to the temple today to declare the word of God to you.
You know I have always been faithful in my calling. But today I am weighed down with even more desire and anxiety for the welfare of your souls than I have ever been.
So far, you have been obedient to the word of the Lord that I have given you.

But listen to me. With the help of the all-powerful Creator of heaven and earth, I can tell you about your thoughts — how you are beginning to fall into sin, a sin that is deeply offensive to me, and deeply offensive to God.
It grieves my soul and makes me shrink with shame before my Maker that I must testify to you about the wickedness of your hearts.
It also grieves me that I must speak so boldly about this in front of your wives and children, many of whose feelings are exceedingly tender, pure, and delicate before God — which is pleasing to God.
I believe they came here to hear the pleasing word of God — the word that heals the wounded soul.

It burdens my soul that I am forced, because of the strict commandment I received from God, to warn you about your sins. Instead of comforting and healing the wounds of those already hurt, I must make their wounds deeper. And those who have not been wounded — instead of hearing the pleasing word of God, they must now have daggers placed to pierce their souls and wound their tender minds.

But despite how difficult this task is, I must obey God's strict commands and tell you about your wickedness and abominations, in the presence of the pure in heart and the brokenhearted, under the all-seeing eye of Almighty God.

I must tell you the truth according to the plainness of God's word. When I asked the Lord, this is the word that came to me: "Jacob, go to the temple tomorrow and declare the word I will give you to this people."

Now, my people, this is the word I declare to you: Many of you have begun to search for gold, silver, and all kinds of precious metals. This land — a promised land for you and your descendants — is extremely rich in these things.

God's hand of providence has smiled upon you generously, and you have obtained many riches. But because some of you have more wealth than your neighbors, you are lifted up in pride. You walk with stiff necks and heads held high because of your expensive clothing, and you look down on your neighbors because you think you are better than they are.
Now, my people, do you think God justifies you in this? I tell you, no! He condemns you, and if you continue in these things, his judgments will come upon you swiftly.
I wish he would show you that with one glance of his eye he could strike you to the dust!
I wish he would rid you of this wickedness and abomination! Please listen to the word of his commands, and do not let the pride of your hearts destroy your souls!
Think of your neighbors as you think of yourselves. Be friendly with everyone and share your wealth freely, so they can be rich like you.
But before you seek riches, seek the kingdom of God.

After you have gained hope in Christ, then you may seek riches — and you will seek them with the intent to do good: to clothe the naked, feed the hungry, free the captive, and give relief to the sick and the afflicted.

Now, my people, I have spoken to you about pride. Those of you who have mistreated your neighbors and looked down on them because you were proud of the things God has given you — what do you have to say?

Don't you think such things are abominable to the one who created all people? One person is just as precious in God's sight as another. All people come from dust, and he created them all for the same purpose: to keep his commandments and glorify him forever.
Now I will stop speaking about pride. If I did not have to speak to you about an even worse sin, my heart would be filled with joy because of you.

But the word of God weighs on me because of your greater sins. The Lord says: "This people is growing in wickedness. They do not understand the scriptures. They try to justify their sexual sins because of what was written about David and Solomon."
"David and Solomon truly did have many wives and concubines, which was abominable before me," says the Lord.
"I led this people out of Jerusalem by the power of my arm so I could raise up a righteous branch from the descendants of Joseph."
"I, the Lord God, will not allow this people to do as those of old."
"Therefore, my people, hear me, and listen to the word of the Lord: No man among you shall have more than one wife. He shall have no concubines."
"For I, the Lord God, delight in the purity of women. Sexual sin is an abomination before me," says the Lord of Hosts.
"This people shall keep my commandments," says the Lord of Hosts, "or the land will be cursed because of them."
"For if I choose to raise up descendants for myself," says the Lord of Hosts, "I will command my people. Otherwise, they shall obey these things."
"For I, the Lord, have seen the sorrow and heard the mourning of the daughters of my people in Jerusalem and in all their lands, because of the wickedness and abominations of their husbands."
"I will not allow the cries of the beautiful daughters of this people, whom I led out of Jerusalem, to come up to me against the men of my people," says the Lord of Hosts.

"They shall not lead away the daughters of my people captive because of the women's tenderness, or I will visit them with a severe curse, even destruction. They shall not commit sexual sins like those of old," says the Lord of Hosts.
You know these commandments were given to our father Lehi. Therefore, you knew them before, and you have come under great condemnation because you have done things you should not have done.

You have done worse things than the Lamanites, our brothers. You have broken the hearts of your tender wives and lost the trust of your children because of the bad example you have set. The sobbing of their hearts rises up to God against you. Because of the strictness of God's word, which comes down against you, many hearts have died, pierced with deep wounds.

3.text: But I, Jacob, want to speak to those of you who are pure in heart. Look to God with firmness of mind, pray to him with exceeding faith, and he will comfort you in your troubles. He will plead your cause and bring justice upon those who seek to destroy you.
All of you who are pure in heart — lift up your heads and receive the pleasing word of God! Feast on his love, for if your minds are firm, you may do so forever.

But how terrible for you who are not pure in heart — who are filthy before God today! Unless you repent, the land will be cursed because of you. The Lamanites, who are not filthy like you — even though they are also severely cursed — will scourge you, even to destruction.
The time is coming quickly: unless you repent, they will possess your land of inheritance, and the Lord God will lead the righteous away from among you.

The Lamanites, your brothers whom you hate because of their appearance and the curse upon their skin, are more righteous than you. They have not forgotten the commandment of the Lord given to our father: that they should have only one wife and no concubines, and there should be no sexual sin among them.
They keep this commandment. Because they keep it, the Lord God will not destroy them but will be merciful to them. One day they will become a blessed people.

Their husbands love their wives, and their wives love their husbands. Their husbands and wives love their children. Their hostility toward you comes from the wickedness of their ancestors. How much better are you than they in the sight of your great Creator?
My people, I fear that unless you repent of your sins, their skins will be whiter than yours when you are brought with them before the throne of God.

Therefore, I give you this commandment, which is the word of God: Do not mock them anymore because of the darkness of their skin. Do not mock them because of their condition. Instead, remember your own sinfulness, and remember that their condition came because of their ancestors.

Remember your children — how you have grieved their hearts because of the example you have set. Remember that because of your sinfulness, you may bring your children to destruction, and their sins will be heaped on your heads at the last day.

My people, listen to my words! Wake up all the powers of your souls! Shake yourselves out of the sleep of death! Free yourselves from the pains of hell so you do not become servants of the devil, cast into that lake of fire and brimstone, which is the second death.
I, Jacob, spoke many more things to the people of Nephi, warning them against sexual sin and every kind of sin, telling them the terrible consequences.

Not even a hundredth part of everything this people did — who had now become very numerous — can be written on these plates. But many of their activities are written on the larger plates, including their wars, their conflicts, and the reigns of their kings.
These plates are called the plates of Jacob, and they were made by Nephi. I make an end of speaking these words.

4.text: Now I, Jacob, had ministered much to my people in spoken word (and I can write only a little because of how difficult it is to engrave words on plates). We know that the things we write on plates will last.

But whatever we write on anything other than plates will perish and vanish away. We can write a few words on plates that will give our children and our beloved people a small amount of knowledge about us and about their ancestors.

We rejoice in this! We work hard to engrave these words on plates, hoping our beloved people and our children will receive them with thankful hearts and read them with joy and not with sorrow or contempt about their first parents.

We have written these things so they will know that we knew about Christ. We had a hope of his glory many hundreds of years before his coming — and not only we ourselves, but all the holy prophets before us.

They believed in Christ and worshiped the Father in his name. We also worship the Father in his name. We keep the law of Moses because it points our souls to Christ. That is why it is considered righteousness for us — just as it was for Abraham in the wilderness when he obeyed God's command to offer up his son Isaac, which was a symbol of God and his Only Begotten Son.

Therefore we study the prophets, and we have many revelations and the spirit of prophecy. Having all these witnesses, we gain hope, and our faith becomes unshakable — so much so that we can truly command in the name of Jesus, and even the trees obey us, or the mountains, or the waves of the sea.
Nevertheless, the Lord God shows us our weakness so we know that it is only by his grace and his willingness to reach down to the children of men that we have the power to do these things.

How great and marvelous are the works of the Lord! How unsearchable are the depths of his mysteries! It is impossible for mankind to discover all his ways. No one knows his ways unless they are revealed to them. Therefore, brothers and sisters, do not reject the revelations of God.

By the power of his word, mankind came upon the earth, which was also created by the power of his word. If God can speak and the world comes into being, and speak and mankind is created — then why can't he also command the earth or anything he has made upon it, according to his will and purpose?
Therefore, brothers and sisters, do not try to advise the Lord. Instead, take counsel from his hand. He counsels in wisdom, justice, and great mercy over all his works.

Beloved brothers and sisters, be reconciled to God through the atonement of Christ, his Only Begotten Son. Then you may obtain a resurrection according to the power of the resurrection that is in Christ, and be presented as the firstfruits of Christ to God — having faith, and having obtained a good hope of glory in him before he manifests himself in the flesh.

Beloved people, do not be amazed that I tell you these things. Why not speak of the atonement of Christ and gain a perfect knowledge of him, just as we gain knowledge of the resurrection and the world to come?

Let the person who prophesies do so in a way that people can understand. The Spirit speaks the truth and does not lie. Therefore, it speaks of things as they really are and as they really will be. These things are shown to us plainly for the salvation of our souls. But we are not the only witnesses of these things — God also spoke them to the prophets of old.

But the Jews were a stubborn people. They rejected the words of plainness and killed the prophets. They searched for things they could not understand. Because of their spiritual blindness — which came from looking beyond the mark — they had to fall. God took away his plainness from them and gave them many things they could not understand, because they wanted it. And because they wanted it, God did it so they would stumble.
Now I, Jacob, am led by the Spirit to prophesy. I perceive by the Spirit within me that because the Jews stumble, they will reject the stone upon which they could build a safe foundation.
But according to the scriptures, this stone will become the great, the last, and the only sure foundation upon which the Jews can build.
Now, my beloved people, how is it possible that after they have rejected the sure foundation, they can ever build upon it so that it becomes their chief cornerstone?
My beloved brothers and sisters, I will explain this mystery to you — if my firmness in the Spirit does not waver, and if I do not stumble because of my deep concern for you.

5.text: My brothers and sisters, do you remember reading the words of the prophet Zenos, which he spoke to the house of Israel, saying:
"Listen, O house of Israel, and hear the words of me, a prophet of the Lord."
"The Lord says: I will compare you, O house of Israel, to a cultivated olive tree that a man took and cared for in his vineyard. It grew and became old and began to decay."

The master of the vineyard went out and saw that his olive tree was beginning to decay. He said, "I will prune it, dig around it, and nourish it. Perhaps it will shoot forth young and tender branches and not die."
He pruned it, dug around it, and nourished it according to his word.
After many days, it began to put forth a few small, young, tender branches. But the main top of the tree began to die.

The master of the vineyard saw it and said to his servant, "It grieves me to lose this tree. Go pluck branches from a wild olive tree and bring them to me. We will remove the main branches that are withering and burn them in the fire."

"And I," says the Lord of the vineyard, "will take many of these young and tender branches and graft them wherever I choose. It does not matter if the root of this tree dies, as long as I can preserve its fruit for myself. So I will take these young branches and graft them wherever I wish."
"Take the wild olive branches and graft them in to replace the ones I removed. The branches I plucked off, cast into the fire and burn them so they do not clutter the ground of my vineyard."
The servant did as the Lord of the vineyard commanded and grafted in the wild olive branches.

The Lord of the vineyard had the tree dug around, pruned, and nourished. He said to his servant, "It grieves me to lose this tree. Perhaps by preserving its roots so they do not die, I can save them for myself. That is why I have done this."
"Go now. Watch the tree and nourish it according to my instructions."

"The natural branches I will hide in the farthest parts of my vineyard, wherever I choose, no matter what you think. I do this to preserve the natural branches of the tree and to store up its fruit for the coming season. It grieves me to lose this tree and its fruit."
The Lord of the vineyard went and hid the natural branches of the cultivated olive tree in the most distant parts of the vineyard — some in one place and some in another, according to his will.
A long time passed, and the Lord of the vineyard said to his servant, "Come, let us go down into the vineyard and work."
The Lord of the vineyard and his servant went into the vineyard to work. The servant said to his master, "Look here — look at the tree."

The Lord of the vineyard looked and saw the tree where the wild olive branches had been grafted. It had sprouted and begun to bear fruit. He saw that it was good — the fruit was like the natural fruit.

He said to the servant, "The wild branches have drawn moisture from the root, and the root has produced much strength. Because of the great strength of the root, the wild branches have produced good fruit. Now, if we had not grafted in these branches, the tree would have died. I will store up the fruit this tree has produced for the coming season."

The Lord of the vineyard said to the servant, "Come, let us go to the most distant part of the vineyard and see if the natural branches have also produced much fruit, so I can store it up for the season."

They went to where the master had hidden the natural branches. He said to the servant, "Look at these." He saw that the first had produced much fruit, and it was good. He said to the servant, "Take some of its fruit and store it for the season so I can preserve it for myself. I have nourished it this long time, and it has produced much fruit."
The servant asked his master, "How did you come to plant this tree — this branch — here? This is the poorest spot of ground in your whole vineyard."
The Lord of the vineyard said, "Do not advise me. I knew it was a poor spot of ground. That is why I said I have nourished it all this time. And you can see that it has produced much fruit."

The Lord of the vineyard said to his servant, "Look over here. I planted another branch of the tree here, and you know this ground was even poorer than the first. But look at the tree! I have nourished it all this time, and it has produced much fruit. Gather it and store it for the season."
The Lord of the vineyard said again to his servant, "Look here — another branch I planted. I have nourished it too, and it has produced fruit."

He said to the servant, "Look at the last one. I planted this in good ground, and I have nourished it all this time. But only part of the tree has produced good fruit — the other part has produced wild fruit. I nourished this tree just like the others."
The Lord of the vineyard said to the servant, "Pull off the branches that have not produced good fruit and throw them into the fire."
But the servant said, "Let us prune it, dig around it, and nourish it a little longer. Perhaps it will produce good fruit that you can store for the season."
The Lord of the vineyard and his servant nourished all the fruit of the vineyard.

A long time passed, and the Lord of the vineyard said to his servant, "Come, let us go into the vineyard and work again. The time is drawing near and the end will come soon. I must store up fruit for the season."
They went into the vineyard and came to the tree whose natural branches had been removed and replaced with wild ones. All sorts of bad fruit cluttered the tree.
The Lord of the vineyard tasted every kind of fruit. He said, "We have nourished this tree for a long time, and I have stored much fruit for myself from previous seasons."
"But this time it has produced much fruit, and none of it is good. There are all kinds of bad fruit. It has no value despite all our work. It grieves me to lose this tree."
The Lord of the vineyard said to the servant, "What shall we do with this tree so I can preserve good fruit from it again?"
The servant said to his master, "Because you grafted in the wild olive branches, they have nourished the roots, and the roots are still alive. You can see that they are still good."
The Lord of the vineyard said to his servant, "The tree does me no good, and the roots do me no good as long as the tree keeps producing bad fruit."
"Nevertheless, I know the roots are good, and for my own purpose I have preserved them. Because of their great strength, they have until now been able to produce good fruit even from wild branches."

"But the wild branches have grown and overtaken the roots. Because the wild branches have overcome the roots, the tree has produced much bad fruit. Since it has produced so much bad fruit, it is beginning to die and will soon be ripe enough to throw into the fire — unless we do something to save it."
The Lord of the vineyard said to his servant, "Let us go to the most distant parts of the vineyard and see if the natural branches have also produced bad fruit."
They went to the most distant parts of the vineyard. They saw that the fruit of the natural branches had also become corrupted — the first, the second, and the last. They had all become corrupt.
The wild fruit of the last tree had overtaken the part that was producing good fruit. That branch had withered and died.
The Lord of the vineyard wept and said to the servant, "What more could I have done for my vineyard?"

"All the fruit of the vineyard, except these, has become corrupted. And now, even these that once produced good fruit have become corrupted too. All the trees in my vineyard are good for nothing except to be cut down and thrown into the fire."
"And this last one, whose branch has withered — I planted it in a good spot of ground, a spot that was my most choice location above all other parts of my vineyard."
"You saw that I also cleared away everything that was crowding this spot so I could plant this tree instead."

"You saw that part of it produced good fruit and part produced wild fruit. Because I did not pull off the bad branches and throw them into the fire, they overcame the good branch until it withered and died."

"Despite all the care we have given my vineyard, the trees have become corrupted and produce no good fruit. I had hoped to preserve these and store their fruit for the season. But they have become like wild olive trees — worthless except to be cut down and thrown into the fire. It grieves me to lose them."

"What more could I have done for my vineyard? Have I been lazy? Have I not nourished it? I did nourish it! I dug around it, pruned it, and fertilized it. I stretched out my hand almost all day long, and the end is near. It grieves me that I must cut down all the trees and throw them into the fire. Who has corrupted my vineyard?"

The servant said to his master, "Is it not the overgrowth of the vineyard? Have not the branches overtaken the good roots? Because the branches grew faster than the roots could sustain, they took the strength for themselves. Is this not the reason your vineyard trees have become corrupted?"

The Lord of the vineyard said to his servant, "Let us go and cut down the trees and throw them into the fire so they do not clutter the ground, because I have done everything I could. What more could I have done for my vineyard?"
But the servant said to the Lord of the vineyard, "Spare it a little longer."
The Lord said, "Yes, I will spare it a little longer, because it grieves me to lose the trees of my vineyard."

"Let us take branches from the ones I planted in the most distant parts of my vineyard and graft them back into the tree they came from. Let us pluck from the tree the branches whose fruit is the most bitter and graft the natural branches of the tree in their place."
"I will do this so the tree may not die. Perhaps I can preserve its roots for my own purpose."

"The roots of the natural branches I planted elsewhere are still alive. So that I may preserve them too, I will take branches from this tree and graft them into those trees — their mother tree's branches — so I can keep the roots alive. Perhaps when the roots are strong enough, the trees may produce good fruit for me, and I may still have glory in the fruit of my vineyard."
So they took branches from the natural tree that had become wild and grafted them into the natural trees that had also become wild.
They also took branches from the natural trees that had become wild and grafted them back into their mother tree.
The Lord of the vineyard said to the servant, "Do not pluck the wild branches from the trees except the most bitter ones. Graft into them as I have said."
"We will nourish the trees of the vineyard again. We will trim the branches. We will pluck from the trees the branches that are too ripe and must die, and throw them into the fire."
"I do this so that perhaps the roots will gain strength because of their goodness. And because of the change in branches, the good may overcome the evil."

"Because I have preserved the natural branches and roots, and have grafted the natural branches back into their mother tree, and have preserved the roots of the mother tree — perhaps the trees of my vineyard may produce good fruit again. Then I may have joy again in the fruit of my vineyard, and I can rejoice greatly that I preserved the roots and branches of the original fruit."

"So go — call servants — so we may work with all our might in the vineyard, that we may prepare the way for me to bring forth the natural fruit again. The natural fruit is good and the most precious of all fruit."
"Let us go and work with all our might this last time, because the end is near. This is the last time I will prune my vineyard."

"Graft in the branches. Begin with the last so they may be first, and the first may be last. Dig around the trees, both old and young, the first and the last, and the last and the first, so all may be nourished once more for the last time."
"Dig around them, prune them, and fertilize them once more for the last time, because the end is near. If these last grafts grow and produce natural fruit, then prepare the way for them to thrive."

"As they begin to grow, clear away the branches that produce bitter fruit, according to the strength and size of the good branches. Do not clear away all the bad ones at once, or the roots might be too strong for the graft and the graft might die, and I would lose the trees."

"It grieves me to lose the trees of my vineyard. Clear away the bad as the good grows, so the root and the top are equal in strength, until the good overcomes the bad. Then the bad can be cut down and thrown into the fire so it does not clutter the ground of my vineyard. This is how I will sweep the bad out of my vineyard."
"I will graft the branches of the natural tree back into the natural tree."
"I will graft the branches of the natural tree into the natural branches of the tree. This is how I will bring them together again, so they will produce natural fruit and become one."
"The bad will be thrown away — out of all the land of my vineyard. This is the only time I will prune my vineyard."
The Lord of the vineyard sent his servant, who went and did as the Lord had commanded. He brought other servants, and there were only a few.

The Lord of the vineyard said to them, "Go and work in the vineyard with all your might. This is the last time I will nourish my vineyard, because the end is near and the season is coming quickly. If you work with all your might with me, you will have joy in the fruit I store up for myself when the time comes."
The servants went and worked with all their might. The Lord of the vineyard worked with them too. They obeyed all the Lord of the vineyard's commands.

The natural fruit began to appear in the vineyard again. The natural branches began to grow and thrive greatly. The wild branches began to be pulled off and thrown away. They kept the root and the top of the tree equal in strength.

They worked with great diligence according to the commands of the Lord of the vineyard, until all the bad had been removed from the vineyard. The Lord had preserved the trees so they once again produced natural fruit. They became like one body, and the fruits were equal. The Lord of the vineyard had preserved the natural fruit, which was most precious to him from the beginning.

When the Lord of the vineyard saw that his fruit was good and that his vineyard was no longer corrupt, he called his servants and said, "For this last time we have nourished my vineyard. You can see that I have done according to my will. I have preserved the natural fruit, and it is good, just as it was in the beginning. You are blessed because you were diligent in working with me in my vineyard. You kept my commandments and brought back the natural fruit so my vineyard is no longer corrupted and the bad is thrown away. You will have joy with me because of the fruit of my vineyard."

"For a long time I will store the fruit of my vineyard for the coming season. For the last time I have nourished, pruned, dug around, and fertilized my vineyard. I will store up fruit for a long time, according to what I have said."

"When the time comes that bad fruit enters my vineyard again, I will separate the good from the bad. The good I will preserve for myself, and the bad I will throw away into its own place. Then comes the season and the end, and I will cause my vineyard to be burned with fire."

6.text: Now, as I told you I would prophesy — this is my prophecy: the things this prophet Zenos spoke about the house of Israel, comparing them to a cultivated olive tree, must surely come to pass.

The day the Lord reaches out his hand a second time to recover his people is the day — the last time — that the servants of the Lord will go forth in his power to nourish and prune his vineyard. After that, the end will come soon.
How blessed are those who have worked diligently in his vineyard! How cursed are those who will be cast out to their own place! The world will be burned with fire.

How merciful our God is to us! He remembers the house of Israel, both roots and branches. He reaches out his hands to them all day long. They are a stubborn and contrary people, but as many as will not harden their hearts will be saved in the kingdom of God.

My beloved brothers and sisters, I beg you with the most earnest words: repent and come with full purpose of heart and hold fast to God as he holds fast to you. While his arm of mercy is extended toward you in the light of day, do not harden your hearts.
Today, if you will hear his voice, do not harden your hearts. Why would you choose to die?
After you have been nourished by the good word of God all day long, will you produce bad fruit that must be cut down and thrown into the fire?

Will you reject these words? Will you reject the words of the prophets? Will you reject all the words spoken about Christ — after so many have spoken about him — and deny the good word of Christ, the power of God, the gift of the Holy Ghost, and quench the Holy Spirit, and mock the great plan of redemption laid for you?
Don't you know that if you do these things, the power of the redemption and the resurrection in Christ will bring you to stand with shame and terrible guilt before the bar of God?

According to the power of justice — because justice cannot be denied — you must go away into that lake of fire and brimstone, whose flames cannot be quenched and whose smoke rises up forever and ever. That lake of fire and brimstone is endless torment.
So then, my beloved brothers and sisters, repent! Enter through the narrow gate, and continue on the narrow way until you obtain eternal life.
Be wise! What more can I say?
Finally, I say farewell until I meet you before the pleasing bar of God — a bar that strikes the wicked with terrible dread and fear. Amen.

7.text: After some years had passed, a man came among the people of Nephi whose name was Sherem.
He began to preach among the people and declare that there would be no Christ. He preached many flattering things to the people, trying to overthrow the doctrine of Christ.
He worked hard to lead people's hearts away. He did lead many hearts away. Knowing that I, Jacob, had faith in Christ who would come, he looked for every opportunity to confront me.
He was educated and had a perfect knowledge of the people's language. He was able to use much flattery and persuasive speech, powered by the devil.

He hoped to shake me from my faith, despite the many revelations and the many things I had seen concerning these matters. I had truly seen angels, and they had ministered to me. I had also heard the voice of the Lord speaking to me in actual words from time to time. Therefore, I could not be shaken.

He came to me and spoke to me like this: "Brother Jacob, I have been looking for the opportunity to speak with you. I have heard — and I know — that you go around preaching what you call the gospel, or the doctrine of Christ."

"You have led many of this people astray so that they twist the right way of God and do not keep the law of Moses, which is the right way. You are converting the law of Moses into worship of a being you say will come many hundreds of years from now. I, Sherem, declare that this is blasphemy, because no man knows such things. He cannot tell of things to come." This is how Sherem argued against me.
But the Lord God poured his Spirit into my soul, and I confounded him in all his words.
I said to him, "Do you deny the Christ who will come?" He said, "If there were going to be a Christ, I would not deny him. But I know there is no Christ, never has been, and never will be."
I said to him, "Do you believe the scriptures?" He said, "Yes."
I said to him, "Then you do not understand them, because they truly testify of Christ. Not one of the prophets has written or prophesied without speaking about this Christ."

"And this is not all — it has been revealed to me. I have heard and seen. It has also been revealed to me by the power of the Holy Ghost. Therefore I know that if there were no Atonement, all mankind would be lost."
He said to me, "Show me a sign by this power of the Holy Ghost, by which you claim to know so much."

I said to him, "Who am I to tempt God to show you a sign about something you already know to be true? Yet you will deny it because you are of the devil. Nevertheless, not my will, but if God should strike you, let that be a sign to you that he has power in both heaven and earth, and that Christ will come. Your will, O Lord, be done — not mine."
When I, Jacob, had spoken these words, the power of the Lord came upon Sherem, and he collapsed to the ground. He had to be cared for many days.
He told the people, "Gather together tomorrow, because I am going to die. I want to speak to the people before I die."
The next day the crowd gathered, and he spoke plainly to them. He denied the things he had taught them. He confessed Christ, the power of the Holy Ghost, and the ministering of angels.
He told them plainly that he had been deceived by the power of the devil. He spoke of hell, eternity, and eternal punishment.

He said, "I fear that I have committed the unforgivable sin, because I lied to God. I denied the Christ even though I said I believed the scriptures — and the scriptures truly testify of him. Because I lied to God, I am terribly afraid of what will happen to me. But I confess before God."
When he had said these words, he could say no more, and he died.
When the crowd saw him say these things as he was about to die, they were astonished beyond measure. The power of God came down on them, and they were overcome and fell to the ground.
This was pleasing to me, Jacob, because I had asked my Father in Heaven for it. He had heard my cry and answered my prayer.
Peace and the love of God were restored among the people again. They searched the scriptures and listened no more to the words of this wicked man.

Many efforts were made to bring the Lamanites back to the knowledge of the truth, but it was all in vain. They delighted in wars and bloodshed, and they had an eternal hatred against us. They constantly tried to destroy us by the power of their weapons.
So the people of Nephi fortified against them with their weapons and all their might, trusting in God, the rock of their salvation. They continued to conquer their enemies.

I, Jacob, began to grow old. The record of this people is kept on the other plates of Nephi. So I end this record, saying that I have written to the best of my knowledge. Time passed for us, and our lives passed away like a dream. We were a lonesome and solemn people — wanderers, cast out from Jerusalem, born in hardship in the wilderness, hated by our brothers, which caused wars and conflicts. We mourned through our days.

I, Jacob, knew I would soon go down to my grave. So I told my son Enos: "Take these plates." I told him the things my brother Nephi had commanded me, and he promised to obey. I make an end of my writing on these plates, which has been brief. To the reader, I say farewell, hoping that many of my people may read my words. Brothers and sisters, farewell.
